# Why "AI Generalist" Beats "AI Specialist" in 2026 (Contrarian Take)

Every other week, someone on LinkedIn tells me I need to niche down. Pick one vertical. Become the "AI expert for healthcare" or the "AI expert for real estate." The reasoning is always the same: narrow niches charge premium rates and the market rewards specialists.

I have done the math on 18 months of client work. The specialists are wrong.

## The specialist trap

A specialist in 2026 is betting their career on a single vertical's willingness to adopt AI at a specific pace. When that vertical slows down — regulatory clampdown, budget freeze, a big incumbent launches a competing product — the specialist's pipeline evaporates. I watched three "AI for legal tech" consultants lose 70% of their revenue in six weeks when one bar association put out a cautious guidance memo. They had no other lane.

The generalist does not have this problem. When legal slows down, the generalist rotates to e-commerce. When e-commerce slows, they rotate to B2B SaaS. The skills are the skills — architecting agents, wiring up RAG, picking models — and they transfer across verticals with two days of domain reading.

## The premium-rate myth

Specialists are supposed to charge more. Sometimes they do. But the premium rate comes from scarcity, and scarcity only holds when the vertical is growing faster than the supply of specialists. Every AI vertical right now is attracting 10x more specialists than the demand can absorb. The "AI for healthcare" LinkedIn profile count has tripled since January 2025. The "AI for legal" count has quadrupled.

Meanwhile, the generalist charges on complexity, not vertical. A tough multi-agent system is a tough multi-agent system whether it runs in a law firm or a bakery. The rate follows the problem, not the industry code.

## What actually works in 2026

Be a generalist who writes vertical-specific case studies. The LinkedIn positioning reads "AI Generalist," but the portfolio page has detailed writeups — one healthcare, one legal, one e-commerce, one solo-founder SaaS. Prospective clients see the generalist framing (signals versatility, problem-solving depth) and then find the case study that looks like their problem (signals competence in their specific vertical).

You get the best of both worlds: the specialist's proof, the generalist's pipeline resilience.

## The uncomfortable part

The reason LinkedIn influencers push specialization is that it is easier to teach. "Niche down" is a clean template. "Become a generalist with vertical case studies" is harder to sell as a course. The advice that gets repeated is the advice that fits the content format — not the advice that works.

If you are getting niche-down advice from someone selling a $500 course on how to niche down, consider the incentive.

The AI generalist era is not over. It is just less marketable as a LinkedIn hot take.
